label: approved
body:
>For instance, Saint Brigid of Kildare, one of the three national saints of Ireland, is generally recognized by historians as having taken on attributes associated with the older, pre-Christian Irish goddess Brigid.

I thought that this interpretation of St. Brigid was on its way out in scholarship in recent years?  Have there been new developments in this area of Irish religious studies?
parent_body:
The notion that there was a stark division and bitter hatred between "Saxons" and Normans in England in the 1190s is largely a fictional invention of Sir Walter Scott's 1819 historical novel *Ivanhoe: A Romance*, which is set in England in 1194 and takes significant liberties with historical facts. I haven't seen the new Robin Hood show you are referencing, but my guess based on what you have described is that it probably draws significant inspiration from *Ivanhoe*.

In historical reality, the English people of the 1190s generally called themselves "English," not "Saxons." Moreover, English and Norman cultures had mixed and blurred together so heavily that, by the 1190s, outside the nobility, there was little to no distinction at all between English and Normans, and, even among the nobility, the line between English and Norman was quite blurred. By this point, well over a century after the Norman conquest, most nobles had mixed English and Norman ancestries. Many of the Norman lords and knights who came over with William the Conqueror in 1066 married English women, or their sons married English women, or their grandsons married English women, or their great-grandsons married English women. Many French-speaking nobles of Norman descent identified as "English."

Additionally, by the late twelfth century, virtually everyone in England would have publicly identified as either a Christian or a Jew; no one by that point would have been openly pagan. The Christianization of the English began in the late sixth century CE and was essentially complete by around the ninth century CE. The Norse who colonized parts of England in the ninth and tenth centuries CE were mostly Norse pagans, who reintroduced paganism to the regions they ruled, but, by the later eleventh century CE or so, even most of the remaining descendants of the Norse settlers had converted at least nominally to Christianity. By the 1190s, there was no significant presence of people who openly worshipped the old Germanic deities in England.

Nonetheless, many ideas, practices, and names that were historically rooted in older pagan traditions lingered, and popular "folk Christianities" adapted and incorporated these ideas. In popular folk practice, Christian saints often took on the roles and attributes of earlier pagan deities; in some cases, popular saints even had the same *names* as the older pagan deities whose roles they came to fill. For instance, Saint Brigid of Kildare, one of the three national saints of Ireland, is generally recognized by historians as having taken on attributes associated with the older, pre-Christian Irish goddess Brigid.

Meanwhile, words of Germanic origin that had been used to describe concepts in old English religion, such as "Heaven," "Hell," and "God," were used to translate Christian concepts. In English, the Christian holiday of Easter, which is known in most other European languages under variants of the name *Paskha*, somehow took the name of the old English goddess Ēostre, who the Northumbrian chronicler Bede (lived c. 672/3 – 735 CE) says in his treatise *On the Reckoning of Time* had a festival and a month named after her, which both took place in spring. The days of the week in English retain the names of the old English pagan deities to this very day.

In twelfth-century England, people across all social strata believed that spirits of the dead could return to haunt the living as ghosts and revenants—an idea which originated from older, pagan belief systems and which did not fit comfortably with official Christian teachings about the soul or the afterlife.

Many English people of the twelfth century CE also still believed in the lesser deities of Old English paganism, such as elves, whom people imagined as invisible, humanlike beings who caused sharp, stabbing pains of unknown origin and mental illness, and who sometimes stole human infants and replaced them with elf babies known as changelings. The brownies and boggarts of English and Scottish folklore are generally thought to be related to older, pagan beliefs in household deities and spirits.

**(THIS ANSWER IS CONTINUED BELOW.)**
post_title: How realistic would be that people in the year 1190, England, would still identify as "Saxons" and still hold pagan beliefs as depicted in the new Robin Hood series?

parent_body:
This is one of the famous invented stories by the Danish writer, Hans Christian Andersen (1805-1875). His contributions paralleled the work of the Brothers Grimm at a time when people were just starting to collect with accuracy the oral narratives being told in rural Europe. Paralleling those first gatherers of folklore were authors who sought to produce literature that imitated folklore. In fact, Wilhelm Grimm (1786-1859) leaned in that literary direction much more than his brother Jacob (1785-1863), who is regarded as the founder of modern academic folklore studies.

In those early decades, the lines blurred between "real" folktales and the invented fairy tale-like stories. Christiansen specialized in producing stories that *seemed* like folklore. Two modern - and brilliant - folklorists (Michael Dylan Foster and Jeffrey A. Tolbert) have coined a new term to describe what was happening: the folkloresque. In their two volumes of collected essays dealing with this new term and its concept (I am a contributor to the second), they explore how there are manifestations in media that are inspired by folklore in various ways. Often what is put forward is so much like folklore that it is often mistaken to be genuine.

Christian falls into this neatly. His story "The Little Mermaid" is often put forward as a real folk story, when it is, in fact, Christiansen's invention, inspired by a traditional belief in mermaids. There are folk legends about mermaids - see my article ["Curses, Vengeance, and Fishtails: The Cornish Mermaid in Perspective"](https://www.academia.ed[user]/Curses_Vengeance_and_Fishtails_The_Cornish_Mermaid_in_Perspective) - but these folk narratives are very different from Christiansen's contribution. Nevertheless, if we ask the average person to name a story from folklore about mermaids, they will likely respond with Christiansen's creation (and the subsequent films by Disney).

"The Emperor has no Clothes" is part of Christiansen's "folkloresque" inventions. It seems like folklore, but it is not. We are not, therefore, attempting to understand the folk wisdom embedded in a fable. What is needed here is literary analysis - what was Christiansen attempting to say as an author of fiction. It first appeared in print in 1837, so it doesn't have a long history and I don't believe we would see changed meaning over time. 

The message pf "The Emperor has no Clothes" seems - at least to me - very clear. Namely, Christiansen was attempting to point out what often happens when someone with bloated power (and physique!) is able to cow his court and his followers into accepting whatever BS he says because they are sycophants hoping for whatever scraps the narcissistic megalomaniac leaves in his wake. This situation, however, is a house of cards, and the fragility of a power structure built on lies and illusion easily crashes to the ground. The catalyst for the great fall from power can be the smallest, slightest thing - in this case, a young boy declaring the truth. We need to keep this in mind should we ever be confronted by a bloated narcissistic megalomaniac who spews lies that are automatically accepted by his followers even when they know the truth.
post_title: Is the fable "The Emperor has No Clothes" a political allegory for seeing through the lies of the emperor? Also, where does this story originate? And has it changed meaning over time, or is the message the same?

I've been feeding a local murder for about a month. Up until a week ago, the sentry nearest my building would make excited calls  so that the other members would leave their posts around my apartment complex to come eat. All were very loud, but would come up to me for peanuts. 

The past week it changed. The sentry will still make the loud "Food is here!" call. But the individual birds would then come up to me with a sort of soft gurgle/click sound. I wonder if they are starting to see me as more than a food source.

body:
We already know that inflammation in the nervous system often comes with depressive symptoms (as seen with periodic depression due to migraines), so this is just further establishing a connection. 

There's a whooooole bunch of different treatments that have been found in treating depressive symptoms, which points towards conditions like MDD being multi-causal. For instance, pioglitazone, a medication used to treat Type 2 Diabetes by modulating PPARy activity has shown success in treating depressive symptoms in patients with mild insulin resistance.
parent_body: So these inflammation baded skin diseases have correlation with MDDs? Huh, wonder what aspect of depression would trigger such events.
post_title: Patients with major depressive disorder shares immune abnormalities with inflammatory skin diseases, most notably the immune pathway that is implicated in atopic dermatitis. Because these skin diseases are treatable, the findings suggest new therapeutic possibilities for psychiatric illness as well.

body: This sounds like collector confusion, not an actual threat. A garnishment sent to a bank you’ve never used is a huge red flag. Get everything in writing, request debt verification, a full payment history, and your current balance. Keep paying what you can, document everything, and don’t panic, sloppy records usually cause more stress than actual consequences.
post_title: Need advice: debt collector gave me weird info about a supposed bank garnishment?

body: The question itself is slightly “why do slow sounds sound deep and fast sounds sound high” and really it’s that quality of sound is a feature of your brain. It’s useful to be able to distinguish sounds of different pitches (is it a baby, is it a threat, etc etc) Your inner ear organ has parts that are able to respond to separate pitches based on their frequencies and your brain uses that information to create the sensation of sound.
post_title: ELI5: Why does slowing down a sound make it low pitched and speeding it up make it high pitched?

Reading through this thread completely validated my decision not to buy this game. I’ve seen reviewers saying even after 100 hours the controls do not become more intuitive. 

This is just a hard pass for me. There is absolutely no reason the control scheme needs to be this overly complicated. If I’m never able to forget there’s a controller in my hand and I have to constantly think about what to press, then you’ve failed as a game designer. 

And considering how many button combinations/steps there are for every action, I don’t see them introducing an option to remap controls. Hope I’m wrong though.

parent_body: Every state gets to make their own rules for state ID's/drivers licenses. After 9/11 the federal government made their own minimum standard called REAL ID. Most states still offer the basic ID/DL, but if you bring additional documentation you can get a REAL ID. You need a REAL ID to fly or access federal buildings.
